summary: WP Google Maps exposes multiple unauthenticated REST API and AJAX endpoints. The most critical issue is the wp_ajax_nopriv_wpgmza_rest_api_request handler which acts as a full REST API proxy accessible to unauthenticated users. Multiple GET routes (markers, features, datatables, geocode-cache, query-nominatim) have no permission_callback. The datatables endpoint accepts a user-supplied PHP class name for instantiation. The nonce check is explicitly skipped for features, markers, marker-listing, and datatables routes. Object injection risk exists via maybe_unserialize on database-stored serialized data. Settings page custom JS field allows admins to store arbitrary JavaScript.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated REST API proxy via AJAX fallback
verdicts.description: The plugin registers wp_ajax_nopriv_wpgmza_rest_api_request (class.rest-api.php line 46) which acts as a full AJAX fallback when the WP REST API is blocked. This handler calls onAJAXRequest() which routes requests to internal handler methods. Routes that lack a permission_callback are accessible to unauthenticated users. The affected unauthenticated routes include: GET /markers, GET /markers/\d+/, GET /features, GET /datatables, POST /datatables, GET /geocode-cache, GET /query-nominatim, GET /decompress. Additionally, the nonce check is explicitly SKIPPED for routes matching features, markers, marker-listing, datatables (line 140-143), meaning even POST requests to /datatables bypass nonce verification.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: An unauthenticated attacker can send POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=wpgmza_rest_api_request&route=/markers to enumerate all approved markers with their coordinates, addresses, descriptions, and custom fields. POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=wpgmza_rest_api_request&route=/datatables with phpClass parameter allows class instantiation (though restricted to WPGMZA namespace).
verdicts.remediation: Add permission_callback to all routes that should require authentication. Do not skip nonce checks for POST requests. Consider requiring at minimum a public nonce for read-only data access.

verdicts.title: User-controlled PHP class instantiation via datatables endpoint
verdicts.description: The /datatables endpoint (class.rest-api.php line 1143-1188) accepts a phpClass parameter from the request, uses it with ReflectionClass to instantiate a class, and then calls its data() method. While there is a namespace check (must be in WPGMZA namespace, line 1169), this still allows an unauthenticated attacker to instantiate any class in the WPGMZA namespace and call its createInstance() and data() methods. The nonce check is skipped for datatables routes (line 140). Combined with the nopriv AJAX handler, this is fully unauthenticated.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: An unauthenticated attacker sends: POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=wpgmza_rest_api_request and route=/datatables and body containing phpClass=\WPGMZA\SomeClass. The attacker can instantiate any WPGMZA-namespaced class with a createInstance method and trigger its data() method with attacker-controlled parameters. This could lead to data exfiltration or unexpected side effects depending on available classes.
verdicts.remediation: Whitelist allowed datatables class names instead of accepting arbitrary class names from user input. Add authentication requirement to the datatables endpoint.

verdicts.title: Information disclosure via unauthenticated marker data access
verdicts.description: The GET /markers and GET /features REST endpoints have no permission_callback and are accessible via the nopriv AJAX handler. An unauthenticated user can enumerate all approved markers including coordinates (lat/lng), addresses, titles, descriptions, links, and custom field data. The fields parameter allows specifying which columns to retrieve (though sanitized against actual DB columns). Filter parameters allow searching/filtering markers.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: Unauthenticated GET request to /wp-json/wpgmza/v1/markers or via AJAX fallback returns all approved markers with full details. This exposes location data that site owners may consider sensitive.
verdicts.remediation: Consider adding a permission_callback or at minimum a map-level visibility setting to control whether marker data is publicly accessible via the API.

verdicts.title: Object injection via maybe_unserialize on CRUD arbitrary data
verdicts.description: In class.crud.php lines 339 and 742, maybe_unserialize() is called on database column values (the arbitrary data column). If an attacker can write crafted serialized data to the database via any write endpoint or via SQL injection, this data is unserialized when read back. Given the WPGMZA namespace contains many classes, this creates POP chain opportunities.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: An attacker with editor/admin access creates a marker with crafted serialized data in a custom field. When any user or the API reads this marker, maybe_unserialize triggers object instantiation. Available POP gadgets in the WPGMZA namespace or WordPress core could escalate to RCE.
verdicts.remediation: Replace serialize/unserialize with json_encode/json_decode for the arbitrary data column.

verdicts.title: Stored XSS via GDPR compliance settings rendered on frontend
verdicts.description: In class.gdpr-compliance.php lines 107-123, admin-configured GDPR settings (company_name, retention_period_days, retention_purpose) from get_option are injected into HTML via preg_replace() without escaping. These values are rendered on the frontend GDPR consent notice visible to all visitors.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: A compromised admin sets the GDPR company name to a script tag. This JavaScript executes for every visitor who sees the GDPR consent notice on the frontend.
verdicts.remediation: Apply esc_html() to company_name, retention_period_days, and retention_purpose before using them in preg_replace().

verdicts.title: Admin-only custom JavaScript injection (by design)
verdicts.description: The settings page (class.settings-page.php line 89-91) explicitly skips KSES cleaning for the wpgmza_custom_js field, allowing administrators to store arbitrary JavaScript. The settings save handler has proper nonce verification and capability checks (isUserAllowedToEdit). This is an intentional feature but means a compromised admin can inject persistent XSS.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: Admin stores malicious JavaScript in Custom JS settings. This executes on every page that renders a map.
verdicts.remediation: Consider adding a separate capability requirement (e.g., unfiltered_html) for custom JS/CSS settings.

verdicts.title: Server-side Nominatim proxy (limited SSRF)
verdicts.description: The /query-nominatim endpoint (unauthenticated) proxies geocoding requests to nominatim.openstreetmap.org via wp_remote_get(). The endpoint URL is hardcoded (class.nominatim-geocode-cache.php line 163), and only the query parameter and country codes are user-controllable. This is NOT a general SSRF since the base URL cannot be changed, but it acts as an unauthenticated proxy to Nominatim.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: An attacker uses the unauthenticated /query-nominatim endpoint to make geocoding requests through the WordPress server, potentially getting the servers IP rate-limited or banned by Nominatim.
verdicts.remediation: Add rate limiting to the Nominatim proxy endpoint. Consider caching responses more aggressively.

verdicts.title: exec() and raw unserialize() from brief not found in source
verdicts.description: The agent brief mentioned exec() in 1 file. No exec() calls were found in the plugins PHP source. The plugin uses maybe_unserialize() (WordPress wrapper) in class.crud.php and legacy-core.php, but not raw unserialize(). These were false positives from automated scanning.
verdicts.exploitation_scenario: N/A - false positives from automated scanning.
verdicts.remediation: No action needed for non-existent findings.
